entry.title: Langchain Hana Cache
entry.content:
Introduction

When building AI agents your first priority is to make this bad boy work. Get the RAG pipeline properly aligned, check retrieval correctness and check the overall answer usefulness. The task is not an easy one when you work with clients that have thousands or millions of documents that must be ingested properly.

Eventually you reach the point where you deploy the agents in production. You enjoy the good engineering results until the next BTP invoice shows up. You check the token usage and realize you need to do something about it because, well, it is a lot.

So you check your traces, and discover that while there is a ton of interaction with the agent, a big portion of those calls are variations of the same questions. Different users, slightly different wording, but the same answer every time. "How do I reset my password", "password reset help", "I forgot my password." Three different prompts, three LLM calls, three times you pay. Same answer.

That's where I found myself after deploying LangGraph agents on SAP BTP with HANA Cloud. The pipeline worked well. The retrieval was solid (I'd already built langchain-hana-retriever for hybrid search). The agent state was persisted (using langgraph-checkpoint-hana). But the token bill kept growing with every new user asking the same things in different words.

So I decided to also built a semantic cache for HANA Cloud, that will enhance the langchain/langraph solution that I'm using with my SAP clients and their rag Pipelines.

The idea

Pretty straightforward: before calling the LLM, embed the user's prompt and check HANA for a stored response to a similar prompt. If the similarity is high enough, return the cached answer and skip the API call entirely. If there's no match, call the LLM normally and cache the result for next time.

HANA Cloud already has COSINE_SIMILARITY for vector search. It's the same function that powers RAG retrieval. Here I'm just pointing it at prompts instead of documents.

  1. User sends a prompt
  2. Embed the prompt into a vector
  3. Search HANA for cached entries using COSINE_SIMILARITY
  4. If there's a match above the threshold, return the cached response. No LLM call.
  5. If no match, call the LLM, store the prompt embedding + response in HANA, return the response

Installation and setup

as usual pretty straight forward

pip install langchain-hana-cache

The package implements LangChain's BaseCache interface. You set it as the global cache and every LLM call in your application checks HANA first. You don't need to change anything else in your code:

import hdbcli.dbapi
from langchain_hana_cache import HANASemanticLLMCache
from langchain_openai import OpenAIEmbeddings, ChatOpenAI
from langchain_core.globals import set_llm_cache

connection = hdbcli.dbapi.connect(
    address="your-host.hanacloud.ondemand.com",
    port=443,
    user="DBADMIN",
    password="your-password",
    encrypt=True,
)

embeddings = OpenAIEmbeddings(model="text-embedding-3-small")

cache = HANASemanticLLMCache(
    connection=connection,
    embedding=embeddings,
    table_name="LLM_CACHE",
    similarity_threshold=0.95,
)

set_llm_cache(cache)

llm = ChatOpenAI(model="gpt-4o")

# First call: hits the API, caches the result
response1 = llm.invoke("What are the reporting requirements for article 12?")

# Second call with similar wording: cache hit, no API call
response2 = llm.invoke("Tell me about article 12 reporting requirements")

That second call comes back instantly. Same answer, zero tokens consumed.

What I actually saw when I tested it

I ran three consecutive calls against my HANA Cloud instance to see what happens. First a new question, then the same question but rephrased, then something completely different:

     ======================================================================
       HANASemanticLLMCache Demo
     ======================================================================

     [1] Connecting to SAP HANA Cloud...
         ✓ Connected

     [2] Creating semantic cache (table: LLM_CACHE_DEMO_85DFB4)...
         ✓ Cache ready (vector dim auto-detected)

     [3] First LLM call (cache MISS expected):
         Prompt: "What are the three laws of thermodynamics? Explain briefly."
         Response: The three laws of thermodynamics are fundamental principles...
         ⏱  Time: 9.46s

     [4] Second LLM call (cache HIT expected, similar prompt):
         Prompt: "Explain the 3 laws of thermodynamics in a short summary"
         Response: The three laws of thermodynamics are fundamental principles...
         ⏱  Time: 0.41s

     [5] Third LLM call (cache MISS expected, different topic):
         Prompt: "What is the recipe for a classic French omelette?"
         Response: A classic French omelette is simple yet elegant...
         ⏱  Time: 8.16s

     ======================================================================
       Results Summary
     ======================================================================
       Call 1 (MISS):  9.46s  LLM called, response cached
       Call 2 (HIT):   0.41s  served from HANA cache!
       Call 3 (MISS):  8.16s  different topic, LLM called

       Speedup on cache hit: 23.0x faster (96% latency reduction)
     ======================================================================

9.46 seconds down to 0.41. That's 23x faster. And the omelette question correctly missed the cache because it has nothing to do with thermodynamics. The thing actually works.

Tuning the similarity threshold

This is the one knob you need to care about. The similarity_threshold controls how close two prompts need to be for a cache hit:

  • 0.98: very strict. Only nearly identical prompts match. Safe, but you won't save much.
  • 0.95: what I use. Catches rephrasings without being too loose.
  • 0.90: aggressive. You save more, but you risk serving a wrong answer when two different questions happen to look similar to the model.

I started with 0.95 and haven't needed to touch it. If your agent handles very repetitive questions (think support desk), you could try going lower and see what happens.

Dealing with stale answers

Once this is running in production you'll think about the next problem: what if the source documents change? If someone updates a policy and a user asks about it, the cache might still serve the old answer. TTL (time-to-live) takes care of that:

cache = HANASemanticLLMCache(
    connection=connection,
    embedding=embeddings,
    table_name="LLM_CACHE",
    similarity_threshold=0.95,
    ttl_seconds=86400,  # entries expire after 24 hours
)

# Manual cleanup when you need it
cache.evict_expired()
cache.evict_lru(max_entries=1000)
cache.clear()

What the cache table looks like

Under the hood, the package creates a single table in HANA with:

  • PROMPT_HASH: SHA256 of the exact prompt to prevent storing duplicates
  • PROMPT_TEXT: the original prompt text
  • PROMPT_EMBEDDING: the vector for semantic matching (REAL_VECTOR column)
  • LLM_STRING: which model generated the response. GPT-4o cache won't serve GPT-3.5 queries.
  • RESPONSE: the serialized LLM response
  • CREATED_AT, LAST_ACCESSED, HIT_COUNT: for TTL and eviction

The vector dimension is auto-detected from whatever embedding model you pass in. You don't have to think about it.

When all this makes sense?

Well, this is not something you add on day one to a rag pipeline. As I told at the beginnig of this article, you add it after your agent is working and you're looking at how to make it sustainable, you have to also check whether you are making use the llm cache capabilities, taking care of cache invalidation, the agent making good use of the retrieval tools, etc... In my experience it pays off when:

  • Multiple users are hitting the agent with variations of the same questions
  • You're on an expensive model and the monthly bill reflects it
  • The answers stay valid for hours or days (policies, regulations, product docs, internal knowledge bases)

It makes less sense when every query is truly unique, or when your data changes so fast that the cache is always stale.

Also one thing to keep in mind: every cache lookup needs an embedding call to compare the incoming prompt against stored ones. If your LLM is super cheap, the embedding might actually cost more than just calling the model.

What's next

This is v0.1.0, focused on LLM response caching. I'm working on two more pieces:

  • Retriever cache: cache the retrieved document chunks so similar queries skip the embedding + vector search step entirely
  • Pipeline cache: cache the full pipeline result, retrieval + LLM together, for maximum savings on repeated queries

Each one targets a different part of the cost. Together they should cover most of the optimization you'd want after getting a RAG pipeline into production.

Conclusion

Building the agent is the hard part. Making it affordable is the next challenge, and it's a much more tractable one. This cache lives in the same HANA instance as your business data. No extra infrastructure, just one more table. I saw 23x speedups on cache hits in my tests and the token savings add up fast once you have real users hitting the agent every day.

Give it a try and let me know how it goes.

  • PyPI: https://pypi.org/project/langchain-hana-cache/
  • GitHub: https://github.com/stubborncoder/langchain-hana-cache

entry.title: Re: Modernizing SAP BW with SAP Business Data Cloud
entry.content:
Hello @swati_gawade 

thank you very much for the clear summary of the current BW status, the vision for BDC, and the available paths forward.
This is a very valuable blog.

@MKreitlein  your input is valuable as well.

We are facing the same challenge: thousands of queries that represent high‑value customer content, with years of development effort invested by the business.

Business acceptance would be greatly improved if we could lift this existing body of queries and continue to refine and evolve it further — even if this approach would realistically cover only around 80% of the queries.

A complete rebuild is extremely time‑consuming, and the business typically does not have the capacity for such large rebuild efforts.

Hopefully, SAP will offer additional options to address this challenge in the future.

Kind regards
Dominik

entry.title: Handling Multi-Source Data Using Custom Entities in Unmanaged RAP
entry.content:
Introduction

In modern SAP application development using the ABAP RESTful Application Programming Model, developers often encounter scenarios where data must be retrieved from multiple sources instead of a single database table.

For such cases, Custom Entities provide a flexible solution. They allow developers to implement custom query logic using ABAP classes while still leveraging RAP features such as associations, navigation, and UI annotations.

In this blog, we will implement a Travel–Booking scenario using Custom Entities where data is fetched dynamically through a query implementation class.

Create Query Implementation Class

First, create a class that implements the RAP interface:

INTERFACE if_rap_query_provider

This interface allows developers to implement custom query logic.

CLASS zcl_custom_entitys DEFINITION
  PUBLIC
  FINAL
  CREATE PUBLIC.

  PUBLIC SECTION.
    " Interface used for implementing query logic for Custom Entities
    INTERFACES if_rap_query_provider.

ENDCLASS.


CLASS zcl_custom_entitys IMPLEMENTATION.

  METHOD if_rap_query_provider~select.

    " Variables used for paging, entity identification and filtering
    DATA: lv_top        TYPE i,
          lv_offset     TYPE i,
          lv_entity     TYPE string,
          lv_conditions TYPE string,
          lv_total      TYPE int8.

    " Read paging parameters from OData request
    lv_top    = io_request->get_paging( )->get_page_size( ).
    lv_offset = io_request->get_paging( )->get_offset( ).

    " Identify which custom entity is being queried
    lv_entity = io_request->get_entity_id( ).

    " Ensure page size is valid
    IF lv_top < 0.
      lv_top = 1.
    ENDIF.

    " Retrieve filter conditions from request
    lv_conditions = io_request->get_filter( )->get_as_sql_string( ).

    " Handle Travel Custom Entity request
    IF lv_entity = 'ZI_TRAVEL_CUSTOMENTITY'.

      " Fetch data from travel table with paging and filter conditions
      SELECT travel_id,
             agency_id,
             customer_id,
             begin_date,
             end_date,
             booking_fee,
             total_price,
             currency_code,
             description,
             CASE
               WHEN overall_status = 'O' THEN 'Open'
               WHEN overall_status = 'A' THEN 'Accepted'
               WHEN overall_status = 'X' THEN 'Cancel'
             END AS overall_status
        FROM /dmo/travel_m
        WHERE (lv_conditions)
        ORDER BY travel_id, agency_id
        INTO TABLE (lt_travel)
        OFFSET _offset
        UP TO _top ROWS.

      " Calculate number of records returned
      lv_total = lines( lt_travel ).

      " Send response back to RAP framework
      io_response->set_data( lt_travel ).
      io_response->set_total_number_of_records( lv_total ).

    ENDIF.

  ENDMETHOD.

ENDCLASS.

This class dynamically fetches data using paging parameters such as $top and $skip.

Define Root Custom Entity (Travel)

Next, define the root custom entity in CDS.

@EndUserText.label: 'Travel Custom Entity'
@ObjectModel: {
  query.implementedBy: 'ABAP:ZCL_CUSTOM_ENTITYS'
}
@Metadata.allowExtensions: true

define root custom entity Zi_travel_customentity
{

  key Travel_id : /dmo/travel_id;
  Agency_id     : /dmo/agency_id;
  Customer_id   : /dmo/customer_id;
  Begin_date    : /dmo/begin_date;
  End_date      : /dmo/end_date;

  @Semantics.amount.currencyCode: 'currency_code'
  booking_fee : /dmo/booking_fee;

  @Semantics.amount.currencyCode: 'currency_code'
  Total_price : /dmo/total_price;

  currency_code : /dmo/currency_code;
  description   : /dmo/description;

  overall_status : abap.char(10);

  _Booking : composition [1..*] of ZI_BOOKING_CUSTOMENTITY;

}

Define Item Custom Entity (Booking)

Now create the booking entity and associate it with Travel.

@EndUserText.label: 'Booking Entity'
@ObjectModel : {
  query.implementedBy: 'ABAP:ZCL_CUSTOM_ENTITYS'
}
@Metadata.allowExtensions: true

define custom entity ZI_BOOKING_CUSTOMENTITY
{

  key travel_id  : /dmo/travel_id;
  key booking_id : /dmo/booking_id;

  booking_date : /dmo/booking_date;
  customer_id  : /dmo/customer_id;
  carrier_id   : /dmo/carrier_id;
  connection_id: /dmo/connection_id;
  flight_date  : /dmo/flight_date;

  @Semantics.amount.currencyCode: 'currency_code'
  flight_price : /dmo/flight_price;

  currency_code : /dmo/currency_code;
  booking_status: /dmo/booking_status;

  _Travel : association to parent Zi_travel_customentity
             on $projection.travel_id = _Travel.Travel_id;

}

Add UI Annotations Using Metadata Extensions

Metadata extensions are used to define how the fields appear in SAP Fiori applications.

Example:

.selectionField: [{ position: 10 }]
@UI.lineItem: [{ position: 10 }]
@UI.identification: [{ position: 10 }]
Travel_id;

Create Service Definition

Expose the root entity using a service definition.

define service ZUI_TRAVEL_SERVICE {
  expose Zi_travel_customentity;
}

Create Service Binding

Create a Service Binding for the service definition and activate it to expose the service as an OData service.

Preview the Application

Key Advantages of Custom Entities

Using Custom Entities in unmanaged RAP provides several benefits:

• Fetch data from multiple data sources
• Implement custom query logic using ABAP
• Enable associations and navigation
• Support Fiori UI annotations
• Integrate easily with OData services

Conclusion

Custom Entities are a powerful feature in the ABAP RESTful Application Programming Model that allow developers to implement flexible data retrieval scenarios.

By combining CDS definitions, query implementation classes, and metadata extensions, developers can build scalable services capable of fetching data from multiple sources while still benefiting from RAP architecture.

This approach is particularly useful in scenarios where data cannot be represented by a single CDS view or database table.

entry.title: Component consumption Reversal in SAP(ERP & eWM) using POD from SAP DMC
entry.content:
Hello All,

I'm working on PoC on SAP DMC and would like to know the feasibility/guidance for the below requirement.

Requirement: During the Assembly process, the operators assemble the components which triggers a consumption message of movement type 261 to SAP which consumes the corresponding serial number in SAP and reflects in eWM as well. now there is a NC logged and a component that was consumed has to be dis-assembled and should be reversed in SAP(currently, we have a custom BAPI built to do the reversal with movement type 262 in SAP by calling that BAPI in custom trx and included it in a Custom built POD, we followed this process in SAP MES).

Now if i want to replicate the same scenario using DMC, do i have any standard API/plugins available which will include this activity in the device history in MES as well as reverse the consumption in SAP. How to approach this scenario.

Any references will be highly appreciated!

Thank You!

entry.title: Re: Currency Mismatch in “Make Bank Transfer” App – House...
entry.content:
Good day @rohini_dutta21 

Thank you for your question.

Please note that the bank country or region GB does not automatically mean that your transaction currency must be GBP.

In SAP’s bank account management, the bank account identity includes bank country or region, bank key, account number and currency, which means the currency is maintained as an independent master data. Therefore, a bank located in London can still be maintained with a USD bank account, and the country setting alone should not force the transfer currency to GBP.

Also note that, in payment processing, the transaction currency must match the linked house bank account currency. If the transfer app is showing GBP, this normally indicates that the process is reading a (your) bank account linkage whose currency is GBP, rather than deriving the currency from the bank region itself.

Based on your description, the most likely conclusion is:

  • This is not happening because of the GB bank region alone
  • The issue is more likely related to which bank account is actually connected to your house bank account that the app uses.

I recommend the following:

  1. Open Manage Bank Accounts and check the bank account that is linked to the relevant house bank account. Confirm that the linked bank account currency is truly USD.

See 3115215 - Message no. FINS_ACDOC_POST094 - SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition - SAP for Me for more details.

  1. Check whether another bank account exists with the same bank details but a different currency (see below screenshot). The reason is, the logical key includes currency, the system can store separate bank account records for the same bank country, bank key, and account number when the currency differs. Review whether there is another account variant that is GBP and whether the house bank account is linked to that record instead.

See the following references for more details

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/186460fdc35a4b64a713da9bb00deb1e/28e8e9d52d2f4e81a1542b49206f98d5.html?locale=en-US

3661641 - Unable to assign existing bank accounts to new company code due to fixed logical key in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition - SAP for Me

  1. Review your house bank’s account connectivity and, if needed, assign the correct bank account there. This is very important validation point in your scenario.

Consult these references for more details

3115215 - Message no. FINS_ACDOC_POST094 - SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition - SAP for Me

3502313 - Message no. FINS_ACDOC_POST094 while posting payments in the currency other than the bank account currency. - SAP for Me

  1. For bank to bank payments, the system also uses currency dependent account determination for bank clearing accounts. KBA 3389599 confirms that you must maintain this setup in SSCUI 102803. This is usually not the reason why you see the initial display in GBP, but it is relevant if the transfer later fails during posting.

To summarise:

  • A USD bank account can exist under a GB bank key. The country does not inherently dictate the account currency.
  • The transaction currency must match the linked house bank account currency during payment processing.

 

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: SAP Fiori Elements Full Tutorial in SAP BTP BAS – End-to-End Guide
entry.content:
Introduction: SAP Fiori Elements is a framework that enables developers to build SAP Fiori applications quickly using predefined templates and annotations. Instead of writing large amounts of UI code, developers can define application behavior and UI structure using CDS views and OData services. SAP Fiori Elements automatically generates the user interface based on these annotations, ensuring consistency with SAP Fiori design guidelines.

What is SAP Fiori Elements?

SAP Fiori Elements is a metadata-driven UI framework built on SAPUI5 that automatically generates enterprise-grade SAP Fiori applications .SAP Fiori Elements provides standardized floorplans that cover the majority of enterprise application scenarios.

One Word Definition (Fiori Element)--> SAP Fiori Elements can be described as a standardized floorplan-based application framework.

Fiori Elements---> ( CDS+ Annotation+ Odata services ) .

Types of Fiori Elemnts:
1. List Report – Displays and filters business data in tabular format
2.Object Page – Shows detailed information about a single business object.
3.Analytical List Page (ALP) – KPIs + charts + table
4.Overview Page (OVP) – Dashboard-style layout with multiple cards.
5.Worklist– Displays a list of items requiring action.

Mandatory Components : To develop and run a Fiori Elements application in SAP BTP that consumes an On-Premise SAP system, the following components are mandatory
1. SAP Cloud Connector.
2. SAP Business Application Studio.
3. SAP BTP Trial Account.
4. On-Premise System Details (For SAP S/4HANA or ECC Systems ).

 

Topic: Complete SAP Fiori Elements Learning Series: I have developed and covered all major concepts related to SAP Fiori Elements and BTP integration.

Please go through the link below to learn how to create SAP Fiori Elements applications. The step-by-step process for developing Fiori Elements apps is explained in detail in the following tutorial.

1.How to Connect On-Premise System to BTP BAS : https://community.sap.com/t5/abap-blog-posts/how-to-connect-an-on-premise-abap-system-to-sap-btp-bas-using-sap-cloud/ba-p/14340330 

2.List Reort:  https://community.sap.com/t5/abap-blog-posts/how-to-create-a-list-report-in-sap-fiori-elements-step-by-step-guide/ba-p/14346960 

3.Object Page:  https://community.sap.com/t5/abap-blog-posts/how-to-create-an-object-list-page-in-sap-fiori-elements-step-by-step-guide/ba-p/14347253 

4.Analytical List Page (ALP): https://community.sap.com/t5/abap-blog-posts/how-to-create-an-analytical-list-page-in-sap-fiori-elements-step-by-step/ba-p/14348144 

5.Overview Page (OVP): https://community.sap.com/t5/abap-blog-posts/overview-page-in-sap-fiori-elements-a-step-by-step-guide/ba-p/14348973 

 

Conclusion : In conclusion, SAP Fiori Elements simplifies the development of SAP Fiori applications by using templates and metadata-driven approaches. It reduces development effort, ensures a consistent user experience, and allows developers to focus more on business logic rather than UI coding.

Thank  You,

B Naresh Kumar Nayak

entry.title: How to Create a Define View in SAP CDS
entry.content:
Introduction

In SAP CDS, a Define View is the basic structure used to create a virtual data model from database tables. It allows developers to select and expose specific fields that can be used in reports, OData services, and SAP Fiori applications and RAP Application.

Define View:

  • Define View is the basic syntax used to create a CDS View (entity) in SAP. It defines a virtual data model that selects and exposes fields from one or more database tables.
  • The view does not store data physically; it retrieves data dynamically when it is accessed.

How  to Create  CDS View:

To create a CDS View, open ABAP Development Tools (ADT) in Eclipse .

Please follow Below Steps:  Right click on  Your  Project  and Click on New and Select ABAP Repository Object

After selecting ABAP Repository Object, a new window will appear. In this window, choose Core Data Services, and under it select Data Definition.

The Data Definition option is used to create a CDS View in SAP. After selecting it, click Next to proceed with creating the CDS view.

In Data Definition, you need to enter the required details such as the Package name, CDS View name, and a short description for the CDS view. 

After clicking the Next button, the Transport Request window will appear. Here you need to select the appropriate Transport Request (TR).

In real-time projects, the Transport Request number will be displayed here. Usually, the Transport Request is created first using transactions SE01 or SE09 in SAP GUI. Once the TR is created, it will automatically appear in Eclipse (ADT) when you create a new repository object.Select the required Transport Request and proceed to create the CDS View.

Real Time: Please see the below  screen short for Your Refrence.

After selecting the Transport Request and clicking the Next button, the Templates section will appear.

In this section, select the Define View template under View (creation). After selecting the template, click the Finish button.

Once you click Finish, the system will automatically generate a CDS Define View template, where you can start writing the CDS view code.

Example Define View:

Key Point:

  • Lines starting with @ are called annotations.
  • The  keyword define view is used to define the CDS entity name.
  • The annotation @AbapCatalog.sqlViewName 
  • The keyword as select from  specifies the database table or view.
  • The section between curly braces { }is called the projection list

Code:

@AbapCatalog.sqlViewName: 'ZDVC'
@AbapCatalog.compiler.compareFilter: true
@AbapCatalog.preserveKey: true
@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #NOT_REQUIRED
@EndUserText.label: 'Define View'
@Metadata.ignorePropagatedAnnotations: true
define view ZNayak_Define_View
  as select from /dmo/travel
{
  key travel_id     as TravelId,
      agency_id     as AgencyId,
      customer_id   as CustomerId,
      begin_date    as BeginDate,
      end_date      as EndDate,
      booking_fee   as BookingFee,
      total_price   as TotalPrice,
      currency_code as CurrencyCode,
      description   as Description,
      status        as Status,
      createdby     as Createdby,
      createdat     as Createdat,
      lastchangedby as Lastchangedby,
      lastchangedat as Lastchangedat
}

Onces  complete  your logic   Activate  and Excute the CDS View.  

Out Put:

Define View Entity:

  • its  same as  Define View.
  • its a new  version view .
  • ABAP 7.55 onwards it is availabe,
  • No need @AbapCatalog.sqlViewName  annotation.
  • erformance is better

A Define View Entity works almost the same as a Define View in SAP CDS. The main difference is that while creating the CDS object, you need to select the Define View Entity template instead of the Define View template.

 

Code:

@AbapCatalog.viewEnhancementCategory: [#NONE]
@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #NOT_REQUIRED
@EndUserText.label: 'Define View Entity'
@Metadata.ignorePropagatedAnnotations: true
define view entity ZNayak_Define_View_Entity
  as select from /dmo/travel
{
  key travel_id     as TravelId,
      agency_id     as AgencyId,
      customer_id   as CustomerId,
      begin_date    as BeginDate,
      end_date      as EndDate,
      currency_code as CurrencyCode,
      description   as Description,
      status        as Status,
      createdby     as Createdby,
      createdat     as Createdat,
      lastchangedby as Lastchangedby,
      lastchangedat as Lastchangedat

}

OutPut:

Define view and Define  view Entiy  both are same  but  define view entity as some more feature are there. 

 

Conclusion:

The Define View is the basic building block in SAP CDS used to create virtual data models from database tables. It allows developers to select required fields, define keys, and prepare data that can be consumed by applications such as reports, OData services, and SAP Fiori apps.

By using Define Views, developers can implement the Code Pushdown concept, improve system performance, and create reusable data models in modern SAP development.

 

 

Thank You,

B Naresh Kumar Nayak

entry.title: How to handle SQL NULL dates in CRUFL function for Crystal Reports?
entry.content: In VB6, the Date type can accept Crystal Reports' internal "null date" sentinel (12/30/1899) so formula does not break. In C#, a DateTime parameter cannot accept NULL. If Crystal pass a NULL value, you will hit "Not enough memory operation" error. If "DateTime?" parameter will be used, the CRUFL function will not appear in the Formula Editor.

entry.title: Re: Category Management with SAP Ariba
entry.content:
Hello Romit & Khushboo,

Thanks for your kind words. Regarding issue of not being able to Publish the Purchasing Categories, request you to check if below points help.
We had faced similar issue. We could see below message in Manage Taxonomy > Purchasing Categories.
Message - This category isn’t published yet.

In our case, it was due to this Error.
Error Text - "Failed to get 'one-mds' service credentials from VCAP_SERVICES variable: no service binding found for service plan '(any)'. Please make sure to correctly bind your application to a service instance of the one-mds service."

We could see this error with 'Inspect' (Right click on page > Inspect > and try to Publish > Then click the Error ‘PublishMultipleCategory’ and check the Network > Response > to get the error).

It was found to be MDS destination setup issue. With corrections there, we could publish the categories. See if the same helps you.

entry.title: ABAP Performance Tuning in S/4HANA Series : Part 2 : The ST05 Playbook
entry.content:
The ST05 Playbook: Killing N+1 Queries and Full Table Scans in ABAP

Series: ABAP Performance Tuning in S/4HANA | Part 2 of 7 | Author: Manish Khanna

A report that fetches 50,000 sales orders runs for 8 minutes. You open ST05 and see the same table — VBAP — appearing 49,847 times in the trace. One SELECT per order header. That's the N+1 problem, and it's the most common performance defect in ABAP codebases. ST05 finds it in under five minutes.

This post walks through ST05 step by step, then covers the four patterns that account for 90% of database performance problems.

 

 

 

Running an ST05 Trace: Step by Step

Step 1 — Open ST05

Enter transaction ST05. The main screen has four trace types. For almost all performance work, select SQL Trace.

Before activating, click Trace Options (wrench icon) and configure:

 

Option Setting✅
Restrict to own user✅Always enable in shared systems
Duration limit60–120 seconds for interactive programs
Maximum records100,000 (sufficient for most programs)
Trace RFC callsEnable if RFC performance is suspect

Step 2 — Activate, Run, Deactivate

Click Activate Trace (F5). The button turns red.

⚠️Warning: Do not navigate away from ST05 while the trace is active. Open a separate SAP GUI session to run the program you're tracing.

In the new session: execute the transaction or program. Do only what you need to measure — don't browse around. Return to ST05 and click Deactivate Trace (F6).

Step 3 — Display and Filter

Click Display Trace (F7). Apply these filters before hitting Execute:

  • Duration ≥ 10 ms — filters out fast, trivially cached calls
  • Statement type: SELECT — start here; add INSERT/UPDATE later if needed

Step 4 — Read the Output

Each row is one database call. The columns that matter:

 

Column What to Look For
DurationTime in microseconds — high values first
ObjectThe database table
OpREOPEN = new SELECT call; FETCH = row retrieval
RecRows returned — high counts need explanation
RC1403 = no data found (harmless); anything else investigate

Double-click any row to see the full WHERE clause, which index was used, and the Explain Plan button.

The Four Patterns That Matter

Pattern 1 — SELECT in a Loop (N+1 Problem)

How to spot it: The same table appears hundreds or thousands of times in the trace. Each call returns 0 or 1 rows. You'll see repeated REOPEN operations.

This is always catastrophic at scale. A program that loops 100 times in dev loops 100,000 times in production.

" ❌ Bad: SELECT inside LOOP — 1 DB call per order
LOOP AT lt_orders INTO ls_order.
  SELECT SINGLE * FROM vbap INTO ls_item
    WHERE vbeln = ls_order-vbeln.
  " process ls_item
ENDLOOP.

" ✅ Good: single SELECT fetches everything
IF lt_orders IS NOT INITIAL.
  SELECT vbeln, posnr, matnr, kwmeng
    FROM vbap
    INTO TABLE @DATA(lt_items)
    FOR ALL ENTRIES IN @lt_orders
    WHERE vbeln = @lt_orders-vbeln.
ENDIF.

⚠️Warning: Always check IS NOT INITIAL before FOR ALL ENTRIES. An empty driver table causes a full table scan — you've just replaced one problem with a worse one.

Pattern 2 — Full Table Scan

How to spot it: The Rec column shows thousands or millions of rows returned. Duration is high. The Explain Plan shows FULL scan — no index used.

Click the row → Explain button. If you see no index, you need one.

" ❌ Bad: ERDAT is not a leading key field — no index
SELECT * FROM vbak WHERE erdat = @sy-datum.

" ✅ Good: filter on indexed leading key fields first
SELECT vbeln, erdat, auart
  FROM vbak
  INTO TABLE @DATA(lt_orders)
  WHERE mandt = @sy-mandt
    AND vkorg = @lv_vkorg
    AND erdat = @sy-datum.

Use SE11 to check existing secondary indexes before creating new ones. Adding a redundant index on a write-heavy table costs more than it saves.

Pattern 3 — Large Result Sets with Late Filtering

How to spot it: Rec column shows 50,000 rows returned. The program uses 200 of them. The filter happens in ABAP, not the database.

Every row crosses the DB-to-ABAP boundary. For large tables, this is enormous — especially on HANA where network I/O between app server and DB is still real.

" ❌ Bad: fetch 50,000 rows, delete 49,800 in ABAP
SELECT * FROM vbak INTO TABLE @DATA(lt_all).
DELETE lt_all WHERE auart <> 'TA'.

" ✅ Good: filter at the database — return only what you need
SELECT vbeln, auart, erdat
  FROM vbak
  INTO TABLE @DATA(lt_filtered)
  WHERE auart = 'TA'
    AND erdat >= @lv_from_date.

💡Tip: Also check for SELECT * fetching all 80 columns when you only need 3. Specify your field list explicitly — every column you don't need is data transfer you're paying for.

Pattern 4 — Unnecessary Buffer Bypass

How to spot it: A table that is set as buffered in SE11 (Technical Settings → Buffering) appears in the SQL trace. It should never be there — buffered tables are served from the ABAP shared memory buffer, not the database.

Common causes:

  • SELECT ... BYPASSING BUFFER in the code
  • Client-independent table accessed without the client field in WHERE
  • Table type mismatch causing implicit bypass

Fix: Remove BYPASSING BUFFER unless you have a documented reason (parallel processes writing to the table mid-read). Ensure client field appears in WHERE for client-dependent tables.

Reading the Summary View

After looking at individual calls, click Summarize in the toolbar. This groups all calls by table and shows:

  • Total time per table
  • Total call count per table
  • Average duration per call

If one table accounts for 70% of your total DB time — that's your target. Everything else is noise for now.

When ST05 Shows Nothing Expensive

If your trace runs clean — fast calls, low row counts, indexes used — the database is not the problem. Move to SAT (Part 3). The bottleneck is in ABAP logic, not SQL.

💡Tip: For S/4HANA landscapes with CDS views, also check the generated SQL in ST05. A CDS view with 7 stacked layers can produce a 400-line SQL statement that the optimizer can't handle efficiently. See Part 5 for CDS-specific analysis.

Key Takeaways

  • ST05 is your first tool — always. Database problems have the highest ROI fix.
  • N+1 SELECT (same table, hundreds of REOPEN) is the most common critical finding
  • FOR ALL ENTRIES requires an IS NOT INITIAL guard — no exceptions
  • Full table scans need secondary indexes — check SE11 first before creating new ones
  • Fetch only the columns and rows you need — push filters to the database

Next in this series: SAT + ST12: Profiling ABAP CPU and Memory the Right Way — finding CPU hotspots with SAT and tracking down OO memory leaks with ST12.

Series Home Page

entry.title: Re: Production order PR issue using sub contract
entry.content:
Good day @prashanth_BS17 

Thank you for your question.

Based on your screenshot, what you are experiencing is not primarily a material master mismatch issue. The key point is that your system is currently processing your scenario as Basic External Processing, because the production order operation is maintained with an external processing control key and the system creates the purchase requisition from that operation. There is a clear distinction from Basic Subcontracting. In Basic External processing, the supplier processes a specific operation. On the other hand, in Basic Subcontracting, the supplier produces the material, and the subcontractor’s purchasing info record references the relevant production version.

Therefore, the first question is not whether Material Group L004 matches in the material master and PIR. It is whether the business process should be modelled as

  1. operation external processing, or
  2. material subcontracting.

This is where KBA 2389178 comes in. It simply confirms that in production order external processing, the system can raise an error message, even if the entered material group and info record material group appear to be the same. In other words, matching the visible material group alone is not sufficient to conclude that the info record is valid for your scenario.

Based on that, the most likely explanation in your case is that the process design is mixed:

  • The material is set up with subcontracting logic in MRP
  • But the production order is trying to create a PR through operational external processing.

So, if your target process is material subcontracting, the use the Basic Subcontracting flow and let the subcontractor specific purchasing info record point to the correct production version. If your target process is external processing of a single operation, then you should keep the operation-based process and validate the info record specifically for the external processing operation.

Here are a few validation checks you should carry out in your system:

  1. Open SSCUI 500223 and verify the value of External Processing for control key YBP2.  KBA 3558196 provides details on the prerequisites for externally processed operation.
  2. In your production order, go to the relevant operation, and capture the External Processing tab. Check Control Key, Cost Element, Production Group or Material Group, Purchasing data fields and Purchasing Info Record reference (if you maintained it).
  3. In Manage Purchasing Info Records, verify how the PIR was created. Confirm whether:

  • Your PIR is material specific or if it is based on material group/product group
  • The PIR category matches your intended process
  • You are using a subcontracting PIR while the production order is expecting external processing operation behaviour

See the following reference for more details

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/0e602d466b99490187fcbb30d1dc897c/49e387576b49ab76e10000000a441470.html?locale=en-US

  1. If the supplier is meant to produce the whole material, then test the standard Basic Subcontracting design:
    1. Keep the subcontracting setup in MRP
    2. Use the subcontracting specific PIR
    3. Ensure the PIR references the correct production version
    4. Let PR creation come from the subcontracting planning flow, not from the external processing operation tab.

If the supplier is meant to perform only one operation, then stay with Basic External Processing:

  • Keep the external processing control key
  • Verify the PIR specifically for the operation external processing check
  • Don’t rely on subcontracting production version logic to resolve the error.

Based on what you described, my recommendation is

  • If you want subcontracting of the finished material, do not drive the requirement from the external processing operation. Use the standard subcontracting design and the subcontractor PIR with the correct production version.
  • If you want operational level outsourcing, keep the external processing control key, but then treat the issue as an external processing PIR validation problem, not as an MRP 2 planning setup problem.

 

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Pre-Exit and Post-Exit Extensions in Standard Integration Flows
entry.content:
Pre-Exit and Post-Exit Extensions in Standard Integration Flows

Pre-Exit and Post-Exit Extensions in standard packages allow you to implement additional requirements within the Integration Flow without changing its status to “Modified.”

It is important to preserve standard packages because:

  • You can receive updates released by the vendor.
  • Triggers do not consume the message quota.

 

Now, let’s examine how to activate a Post-Exit extension through a simple scenario.

 

To activate the extension, you need to configure a predefined Exchange Property value to “true”

Pre-Exit runs before the mapping or before a specific processing step, depending on the extension point defined within the standard iFlow.

Post-Exit is triggered at a specific point within the standard iFlow (usually after the mapping). In some SAP packages can be used to access both the original and transformed payload.

Note: The custom IFlow created for Pre-Exit or Post-Exit may include a Receiver Adapters depending on your requirements.

For the Post-Exit, you define the path of the Receiver Process Direct adapter configured in the standard flow within the Sender Process Direct adapter of your custom flow.

After performing the required processing in the custom flow, you must transform the payload into the format expected by the standard flow.

In the standard flow, there is a filter step after the Receiver Process Direct step. The payload returned from the custom Post-Exit flow must be structured in a way that it can pass through this filter.

By following these steps, you can implement additional requirements in standard processes without modifying the original flow.

entry.title: #COE#RFL01 Question about special tax code for Belgium company
entry.content:
Hello,

I work for a Belgium company, and the accountants have a special request about a new tax code for supplier invoice.

First the new tax code works with cocontractor. The cocontractor works like the intra-community VAT with VAT account on the debit and the credit side for the same amount.

Second, a part of the VAT is nondeductible.

So far, I know how to set up this new tax code but it's not over yet.

Third, the nondeductible amount on the debit side goes to the PNL account, it’s normal. But the non-deductible amount on the credit side must go to the VAT account and not the supplier.

And I don’t have any idea how to configure it.

I tried several different tax code setups by experimenting with percentage on MWS, VST, NVV or with the EU Code or with 2 tax code on the supplier invoice but nothing works.

Has anyone ever encountered this situation?

And can you explain how did you handled this situation?

Yours sincerely,

Teddy.

entry.title: Re: Does GL in HANA should have mandatory Cost centre?
entry.content:
You can define substitution logic so that the system dynamically replaces the default cost center based on conditions (e.g., profit center, company code, or posting type). This requires configuration in Business Rules Framework (BRF+) or extensibility options in S/4HANA Cloud.

Using In-App Extensibility (Key User Tools) or Side-by-Side Extensibility (BTP), you can build logic that checks the profit center of the main transaction and assigns the corresponding cost center to the rounding-off GL.

If substitution is not possible, you can create Custom Analytical Queries that split journal entries by profit center and ignore the fixed cost center for reporting purposes.

Having said that, below are the recommended approach

Step 1: Check if your consultant can configure substitution rules in the Cloud system for this GL.
Step 2: If not available, explore In-App Extensibility to build logic that assigns cost centers dynamically.
Step 3: As a fallback, adjust reporting queries to exclude the rounding-off GL from profit center analysis.

entry.title: Issue with Indirect PR Release Strategy & Workflow for AED Currency in S/4HANA
entry.content:
Hi Experts,

I am facing an issue with Indirect Purchase Requisition (PR) release strategy determination for a specific currency (AED) in SAP S/4HANA.

Scenario:

  • A new PR release strategy and workflow were configured for AED currency.
  • The setup is working perfectly in the Development system.
  • However, in Quality and Production systems, the behavior is inconsistent.

Issue:

  • Workflow is triggered correctly for PRs with AED currency.
  • But no approver is shown in the “Release Strategy” tab.
  • If the same PR currency is changed to EUR, GBP, or USD, the system correctly determines and displays approvers.

Key Checks Already Done:

  • Characteristics maintained in CT04
  • Class and assignments maintained in CL02
  • Release strategy classification checked in CL24N
  • All configurations are consistent and working in DEV
  • Transports moved successfully

Observation:

  • Issue exists only in QAS and PRD, not in DEV
  • This suggests a possible missing value, classification inconsistency, or runtime determination issue

Questions:

  1. Could this be due to missing characteristic values (AED) not transported properly?
  2. Is there any known issue where currency-based classification behaves differently across systems?
  3. Which tables/logs would help debug why release strategy is not determined (e.g., CEKKO/CEBAN values)?
  4. Could this be related to buffer issues or classification not being regenerated in QAS/PRD?

Additional Info:

  • Workflow is triggered, so overall configuration is active
  • Only release strategy determination fails for AED

Any suggestions on where to check further would be highly appreciated.

Thanks in advance!

entry.title: Client Certificate Authentication change for Inbound communication
entry.content:
Dear Expert,

I have notification from SAP about Client Certificate Authentication change for Inbound communication.
What i have done is check from Integration Suite on Manage Security >> Keystore. CMIIW

Whether any other workaround should I check?

"
SAP understands the critical importance of ensuring constant availability of your Integration scenarios. Based on the recent announcement made by the Certification Authorities (CAs) we would like to assure you that SAP remains committed to ensure stability and trust with SAP Integration Suite. 
Certification Authorities (CAs) are removing the Client Authentication (ClientAuth) Extended Key Usage (EKU) from publicly trusted SSL/TLS certificates. Public CAs began phasing it out by default in September 2025, and some CAs may stop including it in newly issued public certificates as early as May 2026, when broader removal is expected across the industry.

This change affects inbound scenarios where external systems authenticate to SAP Integration Suite using client certificates issued by public certificate authorities. 

Impact 

If your inbound integrations use publicly issued client certificates, renewed certificates after May 2026 may no longer contain ClientAuth EKU. 
This can lead to TLS handshake failures and disruption of inbound connectivity. 

For further details, please refer to the related SAP Note: https://me.sap.com/notes/0003711618 

Action Required 

To avoid disruption, please review your inbound mTLS setup and take one of the following actions as a part of your next certificate renewal: 

Recommended: Use SAP Cloud Root CA–issued client certificates via the Destination Service . 
Alternative: Configure a Custom Domain and manage your own certificate trust setup"


Regards,

entry.title: Re: How to Create a Define View in SAP CDS
entry.content: DDIC-based CDS views (DEFINE VIEW, what you're talking about) are OBSOLETE since ABAP 7.55, and you should use CDS View Entities (DEFINE VIEW ENTITY) instead.

entry.title: Re: Mapping Tasks Button Disabled in S/4HANA Migration Co...
entry.content:
This behavior is usually related to the migration object lifecycle and the sequence of steps in the Migration Cockpit.

From what you described, it looks like the issue may occur after completing the “Prepare Mapping Tasks” step, where the system expects certain conditions before enabling the mapping screen again.

A few things you can verify:

• Ensure the migration object status is still in a state that allows mapping (e.g., not locked after preparation)
• Check if all mandatory fields and structure mappings were completed correctly
• Validate whether the data upload step has been executed without errors
• Confirm that the correct project and migration object are being used (since behavior differs across projects)
• Review user roles/authorizations, as mapping actions can sometimes be restricted

In some cases, the Migration Cockpit UI disables the mapping option once the step is completed, and it may require re-triggering the process or recreating the migration object to re-enable it.

Also, since you mentioned it works in another project, comparing configuration, object setup, and sequence of steps between both projects can help identify the gap.

Have you checked if the migration object status differs between the working and non-working projects?

entry.title: Re: ABAP Performance Tuning in S/4HANA Series : Part 1 : Performance Tools
entry.content:
@manishkhanna 

Hi Manish,

thanks for the neat write-up!

I'm usually skipping ST05 because I've found that running SAT right away helps to find database-related issues as well when sorting the hitlist descending by gross %.

One other thing I do for long running jobs is to actually use SAT's option to run it in a parellel session for a few minutes to see what's happening. This may require to first identify the server the problematic process is running on (via SM51) and then open SAT while displaying the correct server in SM50. 

This has often been a quick way to identify issues and to determine where a job is spending its time and whether intervention is needed or not.

What I like about SAT is, that you can quickly jump into the code from the results to "take a look around" in order to see if there's something "interesting" happening.

Btw, the links to the next blog post(s) don't seem to work yet.

Cheers

Bärbel

entry.title: Re: Royalties Settlement - Additional Business Volume Tab...
entry.content:
To answer your question, yes a Requirement Class is essentially a control routine that determines when and how a Business Volume Table is considered during settlement. It acts as a filter: only if the requirement class logic is fulfilled will the system pull values from the assigned business volume table. This ensures that different types of business volumes (e.g., rebates vs. accruals) can coexist in the same profile without interfering with each other.

About CL_WB2_BVTAB_REQ_NO_LIFRO_DA, SAP delivers this requirement class with the description: Without Royalties Recipient + Delta Accruals. It is designed specifically for delta accrual scenarios where:

  • The royalty recipient partner is not referenced.
  • The business volume is meant to capture accruals rather than rebate-paid invoices.

This matches your requirement: accruals based on sales order item net values, independent of royalty recipient.

Please note the key point is the requirement class you assign here will only govern the delta accrual business volume determination. It will not override or interfere with the royalty rebate business volume determination, since rebates reference a different table and requirement class logic.

entry.title: Re: How to delete uploaded Bank Statement in SAP from &#3...
entry.content:
Good day @babita_vijay24 

Thank you for your question.

The Delete button is generally not available for all uploaded bank statements. You can only delete when the status is "Received or Saved". If your status has moved to either "Not Received, Partially Applied", or another process status, you will not be able to delete due to audit requirements.

The recommended procedure is for you to use the Reverse function instead of trying to delete. This will also reverse the associated journal entries, and the bank statement entries are then deleted from the backend tables.

If you are not able to reverse it, then review the bank statement, and check if it has follow on processing or reconciliation dependencies.

See KBA 3616575 and 3712203 - Delete button is grayed out in Manage Bank Statements App - SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition - SAP for Me for more insights.

 

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Re: Acces additional IAS fields of current user
entry.content:
It’s definitely possible. You just need to configure the attribute mapping in your SAP Cloud Identity Services (IAS) console.

Go to Applications & Resources > Applications and select your CAP application. Then navigate to Trust > Attributes. From there, you can add and select whichever attributes you want IAS to pass to your CAP application in the SAML assertion or OIDC token.

Once you’ve mapped them there, your CAP service will be able to pick them up via the req.user object.

Hope this helps!

Regards,
Sachin

entry.title: Re: Emarsys contact deduplication
entry.content:
Thanks @JessicaBecker for your reply. I am still a bit puzzled about this. The video mentions:

"The Unique Identifier could be a Customer ID, a CDP ID, or another unique value that distinctly identifies each individual": 

However, this isn't really an "OR" story, but more an "AND" story.

Every system that you integrate to Emarsys, has its own unique id. Commerce Cloud has a different Unique ID than Sales Cloud. They cannot have the same Unique ID.

SAP Marketing Cloud was able to store multiple Origins with their own respective Unique ID. So then it is always clear: the origin "SAP Sales Cloud" with id "1234" is John. The origin "SAP Commerce Cloud" with id "abc456" is also John. 

A bit further in the video it is mentioned:

"Even if Web Extend and Mobile Engage use different IDs, they can be mapped to a single record to ensure consistent behavioral tracking". 

But I can't really find how to do that mapping of different IDs to a single record.

entry.title: Re: SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud – PR Editing Restriction Aft...
entry.content:
Dear @Keratiya_D,

In Public Cloud a PR is not automatically locked in standard just because a PO has been created from it. SAP Note says that in My Purchase Requisitions New, PRs with a follow-on document can still be edited and if you want to prevent that the recommended approach is to implement Badi MM_PUR_S4_PR_CHECK. The same Note also says partial item-level locking within one PR is not supported by design

More details below:

https://me.sap.com/notes/0003498391

So for first question , no there is no standard universal lock PR after PO creation function in Public Cloud. If the business wants this behavior, it must be enforced via extensibility, typically with MM_PUR_S4_PR_CHECK and depending on the requirement, possibly also MM_PUR_S4_PR_MODIFY_ITEM

For second question. SAP documents that a PR item can still be marked for deletion even when a PO already exists and that this has no impact on the follow-on PO SAP calls this the current standard behawior

More details below:

https://me.sap.com/notes/0003684001

Also, deleting the PO item is not a clean reset mechanism because SAP documents that even after the referenced PO item is deleted the PR can still remain in status PO Created

More details below:

https://me.sap.com/notes/0003662338

Summary:

In Public Cloud creation of a PO from a PR does not automatically hard-lock the PR in standard. Therefore, if the business requires a strict lock after PO creation this must be implemented via extensibility for example Badi MM_PUR_S4_PR_CHECK. Deleting the PO item first is not a universal prerequisite for changing the PR and even PO deletion does not necessarily reset the PR status

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: How can SAP ECC/S/4HANA on‑prem automate SSL/TLS certificate renewal as validity drops to 47 days?
entry.content:
SSL/TLS certificate lifetimes are being reduced globally (398 days today → 200 days in 2026 → 100 days in 2027 → 47 days in 2029).  Source: https://www.sectigo.com/47-day-ssl-guide

For SAP ECC and S/4HANA on‑prem customers, renewing and uploading certificates manually every few weeks will not be practical. I’m looking for SAP‑supported ways to automate certificate lifecycle for on‑prem systems.

SAP Single Sign-On 3.0, SAP Secure Login Server (SLS) can automate certificates today, but it goes into maintenance in 2027, and the successor (SAP Secure Login Service for SAP GUI) does not include Certificate Lifecycle Management.
Are there any other SAP‑supported options to automate certificate renewal for on‑prem systems?

Specifically for SAP ECC/S/4HANA on‑prem:
How can customers automate CSR generation, certificate renewal, and deployment into STRUST / SSL Server PSE without manual steps?

Any guidance, best practices, or experience from customers who have automated this would be very helpful. Thank you!

entry.title: Re: Correspondence SAPV1 (Journal Voucher Vietnam)
entry.content:
Hi, thank you for your question.
 
I checked with the responsible experts, and it looks like the issue may be caused by a missing configuration in the Output Parameter Determination app.
 
To verify this, please do the following:
  1. Open the Output Parameter Determination app.
  2. Navigate to Select Business Rules.
  3. In the Show Rules for dropdown list, select Accounting Document.
  4. In the Determination Step dropdown list, select Form Template.
In the list of rules, check whether the following entry is maintained:
 
Output TypeSender CountryCorr. IdentifierForm Template
INVOICEVNSAPV1FI_XML_ACC_VOUCHER_VN
 
If this entry is missing, please add it manually. This rule is required for the correct form template to be selected.
 
Should the issue continue after adding the rule, kindly update this thread — I’m happy to support further.
 
Best regards,
Vanessa
